title: AP World History: Modern SAQ Practice Exam 10

questions.prompt:
Source 1: Historian Lani Teva, “Navigation and Settlement in the Pacific,” modern historical interpretation, 2020.
“Polynesian expansion across the Pacific should be understood as a major technological and environmental achievement. Navigators used stars, winds, currents, bird patterns, and ocean swells to settle islands separated by vast distances. Their voyages connected people, crops, animals, and cultural traditions across one of the world’s largest oceanic regions.”
Source 2: Historian Mark Ellison, “Islands, Chiefs, and Adaptation,” modern historical interpretation, 2022.
“Long-distance voyaging was important, but Polynesian societies became durable because settlers adapted to very different island environments. Chiefs, kinship groups, ritual authority, agricultural terraces, fishing systems, and resource management allowed communities to survive after migration ended or became less frequent.”
(a) Identify one difference between the two historians’ interpretations of Polynesian expansion.
(b) Describe one specific historical development that could be used to support one of the interpretations.
(c) Explain one way Polynesian exchange or settlement patterns during c. 1200–1450 were similar to or different from exchange or settlement patterns in another region during the same period.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a clear difference between the interpretations, such as Source 1 emphasizing oceanic navigation and long-distance connection while Source 2 emphasizes local adaptation, social organization, and environmental management after settlement.
(b) 1 point: Describes a relevant development, such as double-hulled canoes, star navigation, settlement of New Zealand or Hawaii, movement of taro or breadfruit, kinship-based leadership, terraced agriculture, fishing systems, or ritual authority of chiefs.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid similarity or difference with another region, such as Indian Ocean trade, Norse Atlantic settlement, Southeast Asian maritime networks, trans-Saharan caravans, or Silk Road exchange, using specific evidence about technology, environment, migration, trade, or cultural transmission.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong answer should avoid treating Polynesian societies as isolated after settlement or as only seafaring communities. The best responses explain both long-distance navigation and local environmental adaptation. For part (c), strong answers compare a specific feature, such as maritime technology, environmental knowledge, settlement patterns, or movement of crops and cultural practices.

questions.prompt:
Source: Report from a Russian frontier official in Tobolsk to the tsar’s chancery, 1689. This excerpt is original but modeled on early modern Russian administrative correspondence from Siberia.
“The winter road to the eastern forts remains difficult, and several local clans have delayed delivery of the fur tribute. Some say the hunters have moved farther into the forests; others complain that merchants take the best pelts before the collectors arrive. If more soldiers are sent, the tribute may be secured, but harsh treatment will drive people beyond our reach. We request interpreters, gifts for loyal elders, and stricter rules for private traders, so that the sovereign’s revenue may increase without disorder.”
(a) Identify the likely purpose of the report.
(b) Explain how the report reflects one challenge faced by land-based empires during c. 1450–1750.
(c) Explain one limitation of the source as evidence for the experiences of Indigenous peoples in Siberia.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a plausible purpose, such as requesting soldiers, interpreters, gifts, regulation of private traders, improved tribute collection, or guidance for managing frontier peoples.
(b) 1 point: Explains a relevant imperial challenge, such as governing distant frontiers, collecting tribute, managing merchants, negotiating with local elites, using military power without provoking flight or resistance, or overcoming environmental and transportation difficulties.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid limitation, such as the source representing a Russian official rather than Indigenous hunters, women, clan leaders, shamans, merchants, or captives; or noting that it frames Indigenous communities mainly as tribute subjects rather than as people with their own political and economic goals.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should use the author’s point of view as a frontier official concerned with revenue and order. The report shows that imperial expansion depended on negotiation as well as coercion. Strong answers should avoid assuming that Russian frontier control was simple or complete.

questions.prompt:
Source: Description of a protest photograph taken in Johannesburg, South Africa, 1985.
The photograph shows a crowd of Black South African students and workers marching past a line of police vehicles. Several signs read “End Pass Laws,” “Education Is Not a Crime,” and “Sanctions Now.” In the background are factory buildings and a wall painted with the words “Township Residents Demand Freedom.” A church leader stands near the front of the crowd holding a microphone.
(a) Identify one goal of the protesters suggested by the described photograph.
(b) Explain one cause of anti-apartheid protest in South Africa during the late twentieth century.
(c) Explain one way the protest shown in the photograph reflected broader global pressures on apartheid South Africa.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a valid goal, such as ending pass laws, opposing racial segregation, demanding equal education, ending police repression, calling for sanctions, gaining political rights, or dismantling apartheid.
(b) 1 point: Explains a valid cause of anti-apartheid protest, such as legal racial segregation, restrictions on movement, unequal education, township poverty, police violence, exclusion from political rights, labor exploitation, or resistance to white minority rule.
(c) 1 point: Explains a broader global pressure, such as international sanctions, anti-apartheid solidarity movements, United Nations criticism, church activism, labor activism, consumer boycotts, Cold War debates over race and rights, or global media attention.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should interpret the photograph as evidence of both local resistance and global connection. The signs about pass laws and education point to domestic apartheid policies, while the call for sanctions points to international pressure. Strong answers should avoid treating apartheid as only a South African internal issue with no global context.
